name: Proportionality
description: When two quantities change together in a predictable way (direct or inverse)

content.overview: Proportionality is when one quantity depends on another in a simple, predictable way. Either both increase together or one increases while the other decreases. {{viz:diagram-01}}
content.explanation:
There are two main types: {{viz:chart-01}}

**Direct Proportionality:** y ∝ x (or y = kx)
- As x increases, y increases
- Same constant ratio
- Example: distance = speed × time (at constant speed)
- Example: force = mass × acceleration
- Graph: straight line through the origin {{viz:anim-01}}

**Inverse Proportionality:** y ∝ 1/x (or y = k/x)
- As x increases, y decreases
- The product stays constant
- Example: pressure × volume = constant (gas at constant temperature)
- Example: brightness ∝ 1/distance² (light spreading out)
- Graph: hyperbola (curved)

**How to recognize proportionality:**
1. Set up the relationship: y = kx (direct) or xy = k (inverse)
2. Find the constant k from one data point
3. Use it to predict other values

**Why proportionality matters:**
- Super common in physics
- Makes calculations easy
- Shows that things are related
- Lets you predict one from the other

Proportionality is like a shortcut: if you know the constant, you know everything about the relationship.
keyPoints: Direct proportionality: y = kx (both increase together)
Inverse proportionality: y = k/x (one up, one down)
k is the constant of proportionality
content.whyItMatters: Many physics laws are proportional relationships. Recognizing them makes physics much easier.
